html:
Understanding the Epipolar Setup
Camera 1 observes p1; the matching 3D point must lie on the red ray.
⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
Let's assume we have two calibrated cameras: we know the intrinsic and
extrinsic parameters for both. They are related by a generic
translation, $\mathbf{T}$, and a rotation, $\mathbf{R}$, such that their
optical axis are not parallel. Let's now consider that we see one point
in the image produced by camera 1 and we want to identify where is the
corresponding point in the image from camera 2. The following sketch
(@fig-epipolar_1) shows the setting of the problem we are trying to
solve.

![How can we identify the specific point $\mathbf{p](figures/3d_scene_understanding/epipolar_1.png){width="70%" #fig-epipolar_1}

Are there any geometric constraints that can help us to identify
candidate matches? We know that the observed point in camera 1,
$\mathbf{p}_1$, corresponds to a 3D point that lies within the ray that
connects the camera 1 origin and $\mathbf{p}_1$ (shown in red in
@fig-epipolar_3):

![The point $\mathbf{p](figures/3d_scene_understanding/epipolar_3.png){width="70%" #fig-epipolar_3}

Therefore, $\mathbf{p}_2$ is constrained to be within the line that
results of projecting the ray into camera 2. Remember that a line in 3D
projects into a line in 2D. There are two points that are of particular
interest in the line projected into camera 2. The first point is the
projection of the camera center of camera 1 into the camera plane 2 (the
point could lay outside the camera view but in this example it happens
to be visible). The second point is the projection of $\mathbf{p}_1$
itself into camera 2. As we know all the intrinsic and extrinsic camera
parameters, those two points should be easy to calculate. And these two
points will define the equation of the line (there are many other ways
to think about this problem).

The following figure shows the geometry of a stereo pair. Given a 3D
point, $\mathbf{P}$, in the world, we define the following objects:

elements: Camera 1 center point (C1, black sphere)
Camera 2 center point (C2, black sphere)
Baseline line segment connecting C1 and C2 (dashed black line)
Camera 1 image plane (semi-transparent rectangle with green border)
Camera 2 image plane (semi-transparent rectangle with blue border)
Camera 1 local coordinate axes (green arrows)
Camera 2 local coordinate axes (blue arrows)
Point p1 on Camera 1's image plane (red sphere)
3D Ray starting at C1, passing through p1, extending into space (thick red line)
A 3D target point P on the 3D ray (color-coded, e.g., green or cyan depending on position)
Dashed projection lines from C2 to points on the 3D ray
Epipole e2 on Camera 2's plane (black dot representing projection of C1)
Epipolar line on Camera 2's plane (red line connecting the epipole through projected points)
Projected point p2 on Camera 2's image plane (moving sphere constrained to the epipolar line)
Curve showing coordinate transformation R, T from Camera 1 to Camera 2 (green curved arrow)

interactions.label: Point Depth (Z)
interactions.teaches: Moving the 3D point along the ray shows how its projection p2 moves strictly along the 1D epipolar line in Camera 2, demonstrating the reduction from a 2D search to a 1D search.

interactions.label: p1 X position
interactions.teaches: Moving the point p1 sideways on Camera 1's image plane sweeps the 3D ray in space, causing the epipolar line in Camera 2 to rotate around the epipole.

interactions.label: Camera 2 Rotation (Yaw)
interactions.teaches: Rotating Camera 2 changes its relative orientation (R, T), which dynamically shifts the epipole and tilts the epipolar line across its image plane.

demo_steps.title: Understanding the Epipolar Setup
demo_steps.narration: Camera 1 and Camera 2 look at the same scene from different poses related by rotation and translation. When Camera 1 detects a point p1, the true 3D point must lie somewhere along the red projection ray extending from Camera 1's optical center.

demo_steps.title: The 1D Search Constraint
demo_steps.narration: As we slide the depth of the 3D point along Camera 1's ray, notice its corresponding projection p2 in Camera 2. Even though the point moves in 3D space, its projection is strictly locked to a single 1D trajectory: the epipolar line.

demo_steps.title: Tracing the Ray Projection
demo_steps.narration: Notice how the epipolar line is formed by projecting two key landmarks onto Camera 2's plane: the active ray itself (producing the line direction) and Camera 1's optical center (which projects to the stationary epipole).

demo_steps.title: Sweeping the Ray in Space
demo_steps.narration: If we move the point p1 horizontally in Camera 1, the entire 3D ray sweeps. In Camera 2, the epipolar line pivots dynamically around the epipole. No matter the ray direction, the resulting epipolar line must always pass through the epipole.

demo_steps.title: Adjusting Camera Extrinsics
demo_steps.narration: Let's change the orientation of Camera 2. By yawing the camera, the relationship R changes. This shifts the epipole's location on the sensor, causing all epipolar lines to re-converge to this new projection of Camera 1's optical center.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view positioned above and in front of the two cameras, angled down at 35 degrees, looking back toward the 3D space between the two image planes to capture the intersecting projection rays.
interactionPlan.notes: Represent the epipole by projecting C1 into Camera 2's screen coordinates using Camera 2's projection matrix. Draw the epipolar line as a line segment stretching through the image plane bounds, starting from the epipole. If the epipole is outside the image bounds, ensure the line still passes through it correctly. Use color codes matching the original diagram: red for the ray/lines, green/blue for the respective camera frames and axes.

attempts.html:
Understanding the Epipolar Setup
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Missing the cyan point in space and its corresponding projection on the epipolar line on image plane 2
Missing the projection of the red point p1 onto the epipolar line of Camera 2
Incorrect color mapping: the green point P in space projects to a cyan point p2 on the epipolar line, whereas they should match in color
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactivity and UI, but misses the multiple corresponding colored points on the ray and epipolar line which visually explain the 1D search concept.
action_items: Add a third (cyan) point further along the red ray in space, and project it onto the epipolar line as a cyan dot
Project the red point p1 onto the epipolar line as a red dot
Ensure the colors of the dots on the epipolar line (red, green, cyan) match their corresponding source points in space to clearly illustrate the projection mapping
Adjust the initial camera viewpoint and zoom slightly to prevent overlapping text labels

feedback.rationale: The conceptual structure and interactivity are excellent, but execution needs refinement to add the missing projected points (red and cyan) and correct the color mappings on the epipolar line.
action_items: Add a third (cyan) point further along the red ray in space, and project it onto the epipolar line as a cyan dot

Adjust the initial camera viewpoint and zoom slightly to prevent overlapping text labels

attempts.html:
Understanding the Epipolar Setup
Camera 1 observes p1; the matching 3D point must lie on the red ray.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Image planes have bright green and blue semi-transparent fills instead of clean black borders with white/transparent fills
Extreme label clutter with many extra labels (P_1, P_2, P_3, p_21, p_22, p_23, active P, projection of p_1) that overlap and obstruct the geometry
Thick, prominent 3D ArrowHelpers used for the coordinate axes instead of the thin elegant 2D-like arrows in the original diagram
Cramped layout where Plane 1, Camera 1, and the coordinate axes overlap significantly, reducing legibility
evaluation.notes: Excellent pedagogical interactivity and accurate epipolar geometry, but highly cluttered visually with too many labels, colored planes, and cramped positioning.
action_items: Change the image plane materials to transparent white with clean black borders to match the reference illustration.
Remove non-essential labels (such as P_1/P_2/P_3, p_21/p_22/p_23, active P, projection of p_1) and increase the spacing of the remaining ones to eliminate overlaps.
Space out the cameras and planes more along the horizontal axis to prevent Camera 1 and Plane 1 from overlapping.
Style the coordinate axis arrows as thin, subtle lines instead of thick 3D ArrowHelpers.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactive structure are excellent (scoring 5s), but the visual execution needs styling fixes, label decluttering, and layout spacing adjustments which can be addressed by refining the generation.
action_items: Change the image plane materials to transparent white with clean black borders to match the reference illustration.
Remove non-essential labels (such as P_1/P_2/P_3, p_21/p_22/p_23, active P, projection of p_1) and increase the spacing of the remaining ones to eliminate overlaps.
Space out the cameras and planes more along the horizontal axis to prevent Camera 1 and Plane 1 from overlapping.
Style the coordinate axis arrows as thin, subtle lines instead of thick 3D ArrowHelpers.
actionItems: Change the image plane materials to transparent white with clean black borders to match the reference illustration.
Remove non-essential labels (such as P_1/P_2/P_3, p_21/p_22/p_23, active P, projection of p_1) and increase the spacing of the remaining ones to eliminate overlaps.
Space out the cameras and planes more along the horizontal axis to prevent Camera 1 and Plane 1 from overlapping.
Style the coordinate axis arrows as thin, subtle lines instead of thick 3D ArrowHelpers.

attempts.html:
Understanding the Epipolar Setup
Camera 1 observes p1; the matching 3D point must lie on the red ray.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The 'R,T' green arrow is rendered as a nearly straight tube in the middle, whereas the original has a wide, prominent curved arc at the bottom.
Wireframe frustum lines (pyramid edges connecting cameras to plane corners) are present in the generated scene, but absent in the original figure.
The coordinate axes on the image planes are thick 3D arrows, whereas the original has thin, simple 2D-like lines.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive demonstration of epipolar geometry with high conceptual accuracy, though the 'R,T' arc and camera frustums could be styled more faithfully to the original.
action_items: Adjust the 'R,T' curved arrow trajectory (rtCurve) to curve more deeply downwards to mimic the prominent bottom arc in the original.
Remove or significantly dim the camera wireframe frustum lines to match the clean look of the original figure.
Replace the thick 3D ArrowHelpers on the image planes with thin 2D-like lines for the coordinate axes to reduce visual clutter.
Adjust the default camera target and position to better frame the entire setup, including the bottom curve.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual structure and interaction are excellent, but the execution needs concrete visual refinements including adjusting the R,T curve shape, removing extra frustum lines, and thinning the image plane axes.
